---
title: "Reading per-group z/CTmax off a single quick fit vs. truth"
date: 2026-06-23
format: html
---

## Question

In a sanity check of the new midpoint `by=` feature, a fast 2-group fit
returned per-species $z$ = 5.32 (A) / 3.23 (B) against planted 5.0 / 3.0, and
$CT_{max}$ = 37.90 / 35.95 against planted 38 / 36. Why the gap on $z$?

## Short answer

They are **not statistically distinguishable from truth** — every planted value
sits inside the 95% CrI:

| quantity | planted | median | 95% CrI | inside? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| z (A)     | 5.0 | 5.32 | [4.80, 6.13] | yes |
| z (B)     | 3.0 | 3.23 | [2.79, 3.81] | yes |
| CTmax (A) | 38  | 37.90 | [37.66, 38.20] | yes |
| CTmax (B) | 36  | 35.95 | [35.68, 36.14] | yes |

$CT_{max}$ lands within ~0.1 °C. The $z$ medians are ~6–8% high, but the CrIs
are wide and contain truth — a point-estimate offset within noise, not a bias
that can be claimed from one fit.

## Why $z$ comes out slightly high (contributors, none ruled out by one run)

1. **Tiny, quick fit.** 2 chains × 750 post-warmup = 1,500 draws on a small grid
   (5 temps × 5 durations × 4 reps × 25 trials). Posterior medians carry real
   MC + finite-sample noise at this scale; the CrI width reflects it.
2. **Mild slope shrinkage → upward $z$, same direction in both groups.** The
   midpoint's `temp_c` slope has a weakly-informative `normal(0, 0.6)` prior
   centred at 0. Since $z = -1/\text{slope}$, shrinking $|\text{slope}|$ toward 0
   *inflates* $z$. Both groups biased the same way is the signature of this.
3. **Generating recipe ≠ model parameterisation.** The sanity check planted
   survival with a *fixed* Hill slope and defined $z$ off LT50; the model
   estimates a *free* $k$ and reads $z$ off the relative midpoint $(\ell+u)/2$.
   With $\ell\approx0$, $u\approx1$ these coincide in expectation, but at the hot
   temperatures survival saturates near 0, so the slope is informed by few cells.

## What this is and isn't

This was a single-fit **wiring/sanity check**, not a calibrated assessment. A
point estimate landing ~6% off planted truth on one small fit tells us the
plumbing is correct (right group→z mapping, truth inside the CrI), not whether
the estimator is biased. To quantify bias and coverage, run the project
simulator across many replicates and check the coverage rate and the
median bias — reading one fit cannot separate noise from bias.

```{r calibration-sketch}
#| eval: false
# Sketch of the right calibration (NOT run here; ~minutes per replicate).
# For each replicate: simulate -> fit midpoint by="grp" -> extract per-group z
# -> record (i) whether truth is inside the 95% CrI, (ii) median - truth.
# Then: coverage should be ~0.95; mean(median - truth) quantifies bias.
library(bayesTLS)
reps <- 100
rows <- lapply(seq_len(reps), function(i) {
  raw <- simulate_tdt_2group(seed = 20260622 + i)        # test helper
  std <- standardize_data(raw, temp = "T", duration = "t_hours",
                          n_total = "n_trials", n_surv = "y_alive",
                          duration_unit = "hours")
  wf  <- fit_4pl(std, by = "grp", t_ref = 60, refresh = 0)
  tr  <- attr(raw, "truth_2group")
  z   <- extract_tdt(wf, ndraws = NULL)$z$summary
  data.frame(
    rep = i, grp = z$grp, z_med = z$z_median,
    z_lo = z$z_lower, z_hi = z$z_upper,
    z_true = c(tr$A$z, tr$B$z)[match(z$grp, c("A", "B"))]
  )
})
res <- do.call(rbind, rows)
res$covered <- res$z_lo <= res$z_true & res$z_true <= res$z_hi
aggregate(cbind(bias = z_med - z_true, covered) ~ grp, res, mean)
```

## Takeaway

The `by=` plumbing recovers per-group truth correctly (right mapping, truth in
CrI). The small upward $z$ offset on a single quick fit is consistent with MC
noise + mild slope-prior shrinkage + the recipe/parameterisation mismatch — not
evidence of estimator bias. The gated `test-midpoint-grouped.R` checks recovery
with tolerances ($|z - \text{truth}| < 1.5$); a coverage/bias calibration over
many replicates (sketch above) is the tool for a defensible bias claim.
